title: About
text:
I’m Adam Ezzat — a Data Scientist and Data Analyst based in Cupertino, CA, with a background that spans economics, business analytics, and deep learning. I build machine learning models, design data pipelines, and — in my own time — build programming languages from scratch.
My professional work focuses on forecasting, statistical modeling, and turning messy real-world data into systems that actually make decisions better. My personal work is where I push further: CJC, a deterministic scientific computing language written entirely in Rust, is the project where everything I’ve learned converges.

title: About
text:
Professional Experience

Big Data Insights — Data Scientist
Cupertino, CA | 2023 – Present
Building and deploying ML and statistical models for sales forecasting. I designed forecasting systems using ARIMA, SARIMA, LSTM, and N-BEATS architectures, and developed the company’s core forecasting product around the N-BEATS neural network. I also apply k-NN for historical analog pattern matching and automate end-to-end data pipelines in Python.


Amazon, Lab 126 — Data Collection Specialist, Lab Engineer
Sunnyvale, CA | 2021 – 2023
This is where I learned how much data quality matters — and how hard it is to get right. I ran experiments with audio recording devices and spent as much time diagnosing the data as collecting it. When results looked off, the first question was always: is this an environment issue, a script issue, or genuinely bad data? Tracing problems back through the pipeline — from raw recordings to processed outputs — taught me that clean data isn’t a preprocessing step, it’s the foundation everything else depends on.


Sangu — Data Scientist (Part-Time)
Sunnyvale, CA | 2023
Coordinated data engineering and analytics initiatives. Built PostgreSQL data pipelines, automated log extraction from Linux servers using Perl, and contributed to enterprise CRM data migration projects.


Big Data Insights — Data Analyst
Sunnyvale, CA | 2019 – 2023
Analyzed complex datasets to support product optimization and strategic decisions. Built a K-means clustering model for event location optimization, enhanced data migration with ML techniques, and conducted performance analysis in distributed system environments.

title: About
text:
The CJC Project
Outside of my professional work, I’m building CJC — a deterministic scientific computing language implemented as a 20-crate Rust workspace. It’s where my data science background meets systems programming: a language with built-in tensors, automatic differentiation, statistical distributions, and guaranteed reproducibility. The compiler, runtime, and all core libraries are built from scratch with nearly zero external dependencies.
CJC is in active beta — the full compiler pipeline, dual execution engines, ~300 builtins, and end-to-end ML benchmarks are all working. It’s a serious technical project, not a toy, but it’s not yet production-ready.
You can read more about it in the blog post or on the projects page.

title: Introducing CJC: Why I’m Building a Deterministic Scientific Computing Language
text: 🕑 ~25 min read

title: Introducing CJC: Why I’m Building a Deterministic Scientific Computing Language
text:
What Is CJC?
CJC — Computation Jacobian Core — is a programming language I’m building from scratch for deterministic scientific computing. It’s written in Rust, organized as a 20-crate workspace, with nearly all functionality implemented from scratch using only the Rust standard library.
The language covers the full execution stack: a lexer, recursive descent parser, type checker, two intermediate representations (HIR and MIR), an optimizer, two execution engines, and a runtime with over 300 built-in functions for math, statistics, linear algebra, machine learning, signal processing, and data manipulation.






CJC is currently in beta. The core compiler pipeline and runtime are functional and backed by 5,300+ passing tests, but the language is still under active development and is not production-ready. I’m sharing it now because the ideas behind it are worth discussing even before everything is polished.

title: Introducing CJC: Why I’m Building a Deterministic Scientific Computing Language
text:
Why Build a New Language?
I didn’t set out to build a programming language. I set out to solve a problem: I wanted reproducible numerical results.
In most scientific computing environments, running the same program twice can produce slightly different results. Floating-point reduction order changes between runs. Hash maps iterate in random order. Thread scheduling affects accumulation. RNG state leaks across boundaries. These aren’t bugs — they’re properties of systems that weren’t designed with determinism as a constraint.
I wanted a system where the same program with the same seed produces bit-identical output every time, on every platform. Not approximately the same — exactly the same.
That constraint turned out to touch everything: memory layout, collection types, floating-point arithmetic, random number generation, parallelism. The deeper I went, the clearer it became that bolting determinism onto an existing language would mean fighting the language at every turn. So I built one where determinism is structural.

title: Introducing CJC: Why I’m Building a Deterministic Scientific Computing Language
text:
Core Ideas

Determinism as Architecture
CJC enforces determinism at every layer:

Ordered collections everywhere. BTreeMap and BTreeSet instead of hash-based containers. Iteration order is always defined.
Stable floating-point reductions. All summations use Kahan or binned accumulators. No naive left-folds that depend on evaluation order.
No FMA in SIMD kernels. Fused multiply-add produces different rounding than separate multiply and add. CJC avoids FMA to keep results identical across CPU architectures.
Seeded, explicit RNG. The random number generator is SplitMix64 with explicit seed threading. No global state, no entropy sources.
Deterministic parallelism. Parallel operations are structured so that results don’t depend on thread count or scheduling.

This isn’t a mode you opt into. It’s how the language works. The determinism guarantee is verified in the test suite — for example, the SciML tests train a physics-informed model twice with the same seed and assert that every parameter is bit-identical:
// From tests/sciml_determinism.rs
let r1 = piml_heat_1d_train(6, 20, 30, 0.01, 1000, 1e-3, 1.0, 10.0, 42);
let r2 = piml_heat_1d_train(6, 20, 30, 0.01, 1000, 1e-3, 1.0, 10.0, 42);
for (i, (&a, &b)) in r1.final_params.iter()
    .zip(r2.final_params.iter()).enumerate()
{
    assert_eq!(a.to_bits(), b.to_bits(),
        "Param {} not bit-identical", i);
}
Not approximately equal. Bit-identical. to_bits() compares the raw IEEE 754 representation.


Nearly Zero External Dependencies
The entire system — 20 Rust crates, nearly zero external dependencies — compiles to a 6.4 MB single binary. That one file contains everything: lexer, parser, type checker, two execution engines, optimizer, ~300 builtins, tensor library, linear algebra kernels, regex engine, AD engine, data manipulation DSL, serialization, visualization, and 15+ CLI subcommands. Nearly all built from scratch using only the Rust standard library.
For comparison: Python is ~50 MB (before packages), Node.js is ~70 MB, R is ~30 MB, Julia is ~300 MB. CJC ships the entire scientific computing stack in a binary smaller than a typical JPEG photo collection.
This is a deliberate choice. External dependencies introduce version churn, platform variance, and complexity that’s hard to audit. For a system that promises bit-identical results, every component needs to be under direct control. And for deployment — whether to a server, a CI runner, or eventually an embedded device — a single small binary with no runtime dependencies is hard to beat.


Dual Execution Engines
CJC has two execution paths:

AST interpreter (cjc-eval): walks the syntax tree directly. Simple, easy to debug, good for prototyping.
MIR executor (cjc-mir-exec): lowers through HIR → MIR, applies optimization passes (constant folding, dead code elimination), and runs on a register machine.

Both engines must produce identical results for every program. Parity tests enforce this. If the engines disagree, that’s a bug — and the test suite catches it.


NoGC Verification
CJC includes a static verifier that can prove code paths are free of garbage collection. The NoGC verifier performs transitive call-graph analysis to ensure that annotated functions — and everything they call — operate within reference-counted, copy-on-write memory without triggering GC.
This matters for latency-sensitive workloads where GC pauses are unacceptable.

title: Introducing CJC: Why I’m Building a Deterministic Scientific Computing Language
Introducing CJC: Why I’m Building a Deterministic Scientific Computing Language
text:
Neural Networks Proven in CJC
One of CJC’s design goals is to express real machine learning architectures — not through bindings to PyTorch or TensorFlow, but in base CJC using the language’s own tensor operations, automatic differentiation, and training utilities. Here’s what has been built and verified.

Feedforward MLP (Multi-Layer Perceptron)
The most fundamental neural network — and the one used across multiple CJC demonstrations.




flowchart LR
    I["Input\nx [1, 3]"] --> W1["Layer 1\nW₁ [3,4] + b₁\nReLU"] --> W2["Layer 2\nW₂ [4,2] + b₂"] --> O["Output\n[1, 2]"]


Two-layer MLP with ReLU activation, expressed as struct-based layers with a pipe operator.



Using the same Layer struct and forward() function shown earlier, the key insight is that every weight is a plain Tensor you can inspect, serialize with snap_hash(), and verify for bit-identity across runs. The pipe operator (|>) makes the data flow explicit and readable. In PyTorch, the equivalent nn.Linear hides the matmul/bias addition behind a class; here, the math is transparent.
The benchmarks push this further: a 50-layer deep network (width 64, batch 4) and a 10-layer wide network (width 256, batch 16) are tested with snap_hash() checkpoints every few layers. Both produce bit-identical hashes across runs — verifying that even deep forward passes through random weight matrices stay deterministic.


REINFORCE Policy Network (Reinforcement Learning)
The chess RL benchmark implements a full policy gradient agent in pure CJC — not a toy example, but an agent that actually plays chess, selects moves via softmax sampling, and updates weights via REINFORCE.




flowchart LR
    B["Board 64sq\n+ Move from/to"] --> F["Features\n[1, 66]"] --> H["Hidden\nW₁ [66,16] + b₁\nReLU"] --> S["Score\nW₂ [16,1]"] --> A["Softmax\n→ Sample"]


REINFORCE policy network for chess. Board state and move coordinates flow through a hidden layer to produce action probabilities.









Full REINFORCE forward pass and action selection code





fn forward_move(W1: Tensor, b1: Tensor, W2: Tensor,
                board_features: Tensor, from_sq: i64, to_sq: i64) -> Any {
    // Build 66-dim feature vector: 64 board squares + normalized from/to
    let feat_data = [];
    let i = 0;
    while i < 64 {
        feat_data = array_push(feat_data, board_features.get([0, i]));
        i = i + 1;
    }
    feat_data = array_push(feat_data, float(from_sq) / 63.0);
    feat_data = array_push(feat_data, float(to_sq) / 63.0);
    let input = Tensor.from_vec(feat_data, [1, 66]);

    // Hidden layer: relu(input @ W1 + b1)
    let hidden = (matmul(input, W1) + b1).relu();

    // Score: hidden @ W2
    let score_t = matmul(hidden, W2);
    [score_t.get([0, 0])]
}

fn select_action(W1: Tensor, b1: Tensor, W2: Tensor,
                 board_features: Tensor, moves: Any) -> Any {
    let num_moves = len(moves) / 2;
    let scores = [];
    let i = 0;
    while i < num_moves {
        let result = forward_move(W1, b1, W2, board_features,
                                  moves[i * 2], moves[i * 2 + 1]);
        scores = array_push(scores, result[0]);
        i = i + 1;
    }
    let scores_t = Tensor.from_vec(scores, [num_moves]);
    let probs = scores_t.softmax();
    let action_idx = categorical_sample(probs);
    let log_prob = log(probs.get([action_idx]));
    [float(action_idx), log_prob, float(num_moves)]
}



Why this matters in CJC: This is a complete RL pipeline — feature construction, forward pass, softmax action selection, log-probability tracking, and gradient computation — written entirely in CJC with no framework. The agent manually computes d(log_prob)/d(theta) through softmax, ReLU, and matmul, including ReLU mask construction and chain rule through two layers.
The key difference from doing this in Python: reproducibility is guaranteed. categorical_sample() uses CJC’s seeded SplitMix64 RNG. The softmax goes through Kahan summation. Running cjc run chess_agent.cjc --seed 42 produces the exact same game, the exact same moves, the exact same weight updates, every time. 49 tests verify this. In PyTorch, achieving this level of reproducibility requires torch.manual_seed(), torch.use_deterministic_algorithms(True), disabling CUDA non-determinism, and even then floating-point reduction order may vary.


Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN)
CJC’s AD engine supports PINNs — neural networks that learn to satisfy differential equations, not just fit data.




flowchart TB
    X["Position x"] --> N["MLP [1→32→32→1]\ntanh activation"] --> U["Prediction u_hat"]
    U --> DL["Data Loss\nMSE"]
    U --> PL["Physics Loss\nu_xx + u ≈ 0"]
    U --> BL["Boundary Loss\nu(0)=0, u(pi)=0"]
    DL --> T["Total Loss"]
    PL --> T
    BL --> T
    T -.->|"Adam optimizer"| N


PINN for the harmonic oscillator. The composite loss combines data fitting, PDE residual, and boundary conditions.



The harmonic oscillator example uses a [1, 32, 32, 1] MLP with tanh activation trained to solve u_xx + u = 0:

Data loss: MSE against observed points (optionally noisy)
Physics loss: the PDE residual u_xx + u computed via finite differences through the network, penalized toward zero
Boundary loss: u(0) and u(π) enforced to match known boundary conditions
Total loss: data + physics_weight * physics + boundary_weight * boundary
Optimizer: Adam with learning rate scheduling

The exact solution is sin(x), and the trained network converges to it with verified L2 error below tolerance.
Why this matters in CJC: PINNs are a case where determinism isn’t just nice — it’s scientifically necessary. When you’re claiming a network satisfies a PDE, you need to reproduce the training run exactly to verify the claim. CJC’s deterministic AD engine, seeded RNG, and Kahan-summed loss computations mean the verification is airtight. The test suite trains the same PINN twice with seed 42 and asserts that every parameter is bit-identical using to_bits() comparison on the raw IEEE 754 representation.


Physics-Informed ML (PIML)
A simpler but equally rigorous approach: polynomial regression with a physics residual penalty, applied to the 1D heat equation u_xx = f(x).




flowchart LR
    C["Polynomial Model\nc₀, c₁, ..., cₙ"] --> D["Data Loss\nMSE vs observed"]
    C --> P["Physics Loss\nu_xx − f(x) ≈ 0"]
    C --> B["Boundary Loss\nu(0)=0, u(1)=0"]


PIML for the 1D heat equation. Polynomial coefficients are optimized to fit data while satisfying the PDE.



The model learns polynomial coefficients that simultaneously fit noisy observations and satisfy the PDE. Boundary conditions are enforced through weighted penalty terms. The exact solution is sin(πx), and the optimized polynomial converges to it.


Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN)
CJC’s tensor runtime includes native 1D and 2D convolution, maxpooling, and the full pipeline for building convolutional architectures.




flowchart LR
    I["Input\n[4,3,28,28]"] --> C["Conv2D\n32 filters 3x3"] --> R["ReLU"] --> BN["BatchNorm"] --> MP["MaxPool2D\n2x2"] --> O["Output\n[4,32,13,13]"]


CNN pipeline in CJC. Native conv2d, ReLU, batch normalization, and maxpooling — all deterministic.



// 2D convolution: batch of images through learned filters
let input = Tensor.randn([4, 3, 28, 28]);   // batch=4, channels=3, 28x28
let filters = Tensor.randn([32, 3, 3, 3]);  // 32 output channels, 3x3 kernel
let bias = Tensor.zeros([32]);
let conv_out = input.conv2d(filters, bias, 1);   // -> [4, 32, 26, 26]

// Activation + normalization + pooling
let activated = conv_out.relu();
let pooled = activated.maxpool2d(2, 2);          // -> [4, 32, 13, 13]
CJC also supports 1D convolution with circular padding (conv1d_circular) for signal processing, and the full set of CNN building blocks: batch_norm, dropout_mask, arbitrary strides, and multi-channel operations.
Why this matters in CJC: Convolutions are one of the most numerically sensitive operations in deep learning — small floating-point differences in the kernel computation compound across spatial dimensions. CJC’s convolution implementation uses the same deterministic accumulation as everything else (Kahan summation over the kernel window), which means a CNN forward pass produces identical feature maps on every run. In frameworks like PyTorch, torch.nn.Conv2d on CUDA can produce different results between runs unless you explicitly opt into deterministic mode — and even then, performance suffers.


Attention Mechanism (Transformer Building Block)
Scaled dot-product attention — the core primitive of transformer architectures — is implemented as a native tensor operation.




flowchart LR
    Q["Q [batch, seq, d_k]"] --> MM["Q · K^T"]
    K["K [batch, seq, d_k]"] --> MM
    MM --> SC["÷ sqrt(d_k)"] --> SM["Softmax"] --> MV["× V"]
    V["V [batch, seq, d_k]"] --> MV
    MV --> O["Output\n[batch, seq, d_k]"]


Scaled dot-product attention — softmax(QK^T / sqrt(d_k)) * V — as a single native CJC operation.



// Scaled dot-product attention: softmax(Q @ K^T / sqrt(d_k)) @ V
let Q = Tensor.randn([2, 8, 64]);   // [batch, seq_len, d_k]
let K = Tensor.randn([2, 8, 64]);
let V = Tensor.randn([2, 8, 64]);
let output = attention(Q, K, V);      // -> [2, 8, 64]
This computes softmax(QK^T / sqrt(d_k)) * V with batch matrix multiplication and deterministic softmax. It’s the core primitive you’d need to build transformer-style architectures.
Why this matters in CJC: Attention involves two matrix multiplications and a softmax — three operations where floating-point non-determinism typically creeps in. The softmax denominator is a sum over exponentials that’s sensitive to evaluation order. CJC’s implementation uses Kahan-summed softmax and deterministic batch matmul, producing identical attention weights on every run. For research applications where you need to compare attention patterns between model variants, this level of reproducibility is essential.


Deep Network Stress Tests
Beyond the architecture-specific examples above, CJC includes benchmark programs that push neural network forward passes to realistic scale:











Benchmark
Depth
Width
Batch
Parameters per Layer
Verified




bench_nn_deep
50 layers
64
4
4,160
Bit-identical across runs via snap_hash


bench_nn_wide
10 layers
256
16
65,792
Bit-identical across runs via snap_hash



Both benchmarks insert snap_hash() checkpoints every few layers, producing cryptographic hashes of intermediate activations. Running twice with --seed 42 produces identical hashes at every checkpoint — proving that determinism holds even through deep chains of matmul → bias → ReLU → normalization.


What This Proves
These aren’t theoretical capabilities. Each architecture has been implemented, run through both execution engines, and verified with assertions. The REINFORCE agent plays chess. The PINN converges to the analytical solution. The CNN processes real tensor shapes. The deep network benchmarks verify hash-level determinism through 50 sequential layers.
And all of them produce bit-identical results across runs — something no mainstream ML framework guarantees without significant configuration overhead and performance trade-offs. In CJC, determinism isn’t an opt-in mode. It’s how computation works.

title: Introducing CJC: Why I’m Building a Deterministic Scientific Computing Language
text:
Beyond Neural Networks
CJC isn’t just a machine learning language. The numerical infrastructure supports a much broader range of scientific computing work — all with the same determinism guarantees.







Domain
Key Capabilities




ODE/PDE Solvers
Euler, RK4, adaptive RK45, 1D Laplacian, diffusion stepping, adjoint sensitivity


Optimization
Gradient descent, BFGS, L-BFGS, Nelder-Mead, bisection, Brent, Newton, secant


Regression
OLS, weighted least squares, logistic regression, polynomial fitting


Clustering
K-means (k-means++ init), DBSCAN, agglomerative (4 linkage methods), PCA


Time Series
ACF, PACF, EWMA, seasonal decomposition, stationarity tests (ADF, KPSS, Phillips-Perron)


Interpolation
Cubic natural splines, linear, nearest-neighbor, Horner polynomial evaluation



As a concrete example, here’s an adaptive ODE solver stepping through a system:

Adaptive RK45 integration with step size control

let result = ode_solve_rk45(
    |t: f64, y: f64| -0.5 * y,   // dy/dt = -0.5y (exponential decay)
    0.0,                           // t_start
    10.0,                          // t_end
    1.0,                           // y_initial
    1e-6                           // tolerance
);
print("Final y:", result);         // ~0.00674 (matches exp(-5))
Every solver uses Kahan-summed intermediate steps and seeded RNG for stochastic methods — deterministic by construction.

title: Introducing CJC: Why I’m Building a Deterministic Scientific Computing Language
section: What’s Native — and Why That Matters
text:
What’s Native — and Why That Matters
Everything mentioned above runs inside a single 6.4 MB binary with nearly zero external dependencies. That’s unusual, and it’s worth explaining why.








Component
What It Does
Why Native Matters




Regex (cjc-regex)
Thompson NFA engine — O(n) linear time, no backtracking
Eliminates ReDoS vulnerabilities and supply chain risk


RNG (SplitMix64)
Seeded, explicit state, fork() for parallel streams
Same seed = same output on every platform, always


Visualization (cjc-vizor)
Grammar-of-graphics with SVG/BMP output
No subprocess, no JS engine, no network calls


DataFrame (cjc-data)
73+ tidyverse-style ops, lazy eval, typed columns
Grouping and aggregation inherit determinism automatically



Two components deserve deeper explanation because they’re foundational to CJC’s determinism guarantee:

Native Serialization with Content Hashing
CJC’s serialization format (cjc-snap) produces canonical binary representations with SHA-256 content hashing — hand-rolled, zero dependencies. The same value always produces the same bytes, regardless of insertion order or reference identity. If you checkpoint a model and restore it, the restore is exact. This is what makes snap_hash() verification possible throughout the test suite.


Native Accumulation (Kahan and Binned Summation)
Every floating-point reduction in CJC goes through either Kahan compensated summation or binned accumulators — tensor reductions, statistical functions, loss computations, gradient accumulation, all of it. Naive sum += x loses precision as arrays grow and produces different results depending on evaluation order. Kahan summation maintains accuracy to near full IEEE 754 precision. Binned accumulators go further, providing reproducible results independent of summation order. This is the mechanism that makes bit-identical determinism possible at the floating-point level.


The Compound Benefit
Any one of these components in isolation would be a nice feature. Together, they create something more: a system where you can load data (CSV), manipulate it (DataFrame), fit a model (regression/clustering/neural network), differentiate through it (AD engine), visualize the results (Vizor), serialize the checkpoint (Snap), and know that every step produced deterministic, bit-identical output — all within a single 6.4 MB binary that you can copy to any machine and run.
No pip install. No npm install. No version conflicts. No “works on my machine.” Just the binary, the source file, and the seed.

title: Introducing CJC: Why I’m Building a Deterministic Scientific Computing Language
text:
The CLI Toolchain
CJC ships with 15+ CLI subcommands:
cjc run program.cjc              # execute a program
cjc run program.cjc --seed 42    # explicit RNG seed
cjc run program.cjc --mir-opt    # MIR-optimized execution
cjc check program.cjc            # type-check without running
cjc repl                         # interactive REPL
cjc trace program.cjc            # execution tracing
cjc bench program.cjc            # benchmarking
cjc doctor                       # health/validation checks
Additional commands include inspect, schema, flow (control flow graphs), drift (determinism drift detection), mem (memory profiling), pack (binary serialization), and forge (code generation).

title: Introducing CJC: Why I’m Building a Deterministic Scientific Computing Language
section: Why CJC Is Still in Beta
text:
Why CJC Is Still in Beta
The core is solid: the compiler pipeline works end-to-end, both execution engines agree, the test suite is extensive, and the runtime covers a wide surface area. But several areas need more work:

Type inference is limited — most types require explicit annotation
Generics are parsed but not fully enforced at the type level
The module system exists but needs refinement for multi-file projects
REPL lacks readline support
Optimizer has room for more passes beyond constant folding and DCE
Documentation is sparse outside of test coverage

I’m not rushing to call this production-ready. The point is to build something correct first, then make it convenient. CJC is in a state where it can express real programs and produce trustworthy results, but the developer experience still has rough edges.

title: Introducing CJC: Why I’m Building a Deterministic Scientific Computing Language
text:
What Comes Next
The near-term roadmap includes:

If-expressions: converting if from a statement to an expression (let x = if cond { a } else { b })
Default parameters and variadic functions: making function signatures more flexible
MIR-integrated autodiff: ensuring gradient computation works through the optimized execution path
Module system improvements: reliable multi-file program support
Solver infrastructure: ODE and PDE solver primitives for numerical libraries to build on
Sparse linear algebra: eigenvalue solvers (Lanczos, Arnoldi) with deterministic iteration

Longer term, I’m interested in what it means to run a fully deterministic, zero-dependency numerical language on resource-constrained hardware. The architecture is designed with that possibility in mind, but it’s a future direction, not a current capability.

title: Introducing CJC: Why I’m Building a Deterministic Scientific Computing Language
text:
Why Share This Now?
CJC isn’t finished, but the ideas behind it are clear enough to discuss. Deterministic scientific computing is a real problem. Building the entire stack from scratch is an unusual approach. And the results so far — a 6.4 MB binary containing an entire scientific computing language, 5,300+ tests passing, end-to-end ML benchmarks, bit-identical execution — suggest the approach is viable.
If you’re interested in compiler design, numerical methods, reproducible computing, or just the experience of building a language from the ground up, I’ll be writing more here as the project develops.
Next up: I’ll write about what it took to build a dual-engine compiler pipeline in Rust — and the design decisions that made parity testing between an AST interpreter and a register machine actually tractable.






Follow the project



CJC is open source and under active development. The project is in beta — functional and tested, but not yet production-ready. Follow @adamezzat for updates as new features land.

title: Adam Ezzat
text: Data Scientist. Language Designer. Builder of things from scratch.

title: Adam Ezzat
text:
The Path Here
I didn’t start in computer science. I started in economics — studying systems, incentives, and how decisions get made under uncertainty. That curiosity about modeling the real world pulled me toward data, and data pulled me toward code.
My first real technical work was in operations analytics — SQL queries, Excel models, the kind of work where you learn that most business problems are really data problems in disguise. From there I moved into data science proper: building forecasting models, automating pipelines, and learning that the gap between “a model that works in a notebook” and “a system that works in production” is where most of the real engineering lives.
At Amazon Lab 126, I ran experiments with audio recording devices and quickly learned that the hardest part of data work isn’t the modeling — it’s figuring out why your data looks wrong. Is it an environment issue? A script bug? A device misconfiguration? Tracing problems through the full pipeline taught me that clean data isn’t a preprocessing step — it’s the foundation everything else depends on.
At Big Data Insights, I went deeper into ML — designing and deploying forecasting systems using ARIMA, SARIMA, LSTM, and eventually N-BEATS neural architectures. I built the company’s core forecasting product and learned firsthand how much of “data science” is really about building reliable, reproducible pipelines that other people can trust.

title: Adam Ezzat
section: The Question That Led to CJC
text:
The Question That Led to CJC
Somewhere along the way, a question started bothering me: why is reproducibility so hard?
Every data scientist knows the feeling. You train a model, get great results, and then can’t reproduce them three days later. Different library versions, different floating-point behavior across platforms, hash map iteration order changing between runs. The tools we use are powerful, but they weren’t designed with determinism as a priority.
I wanted to understand what it would take to build a computing environment where the same program with the same seed produces exactly the same output — not approximately, not within tolerance, but bit-identical — every single time.
That question became CJC: a programming language I’m building from scratch in Rust, designed from the ground up for deterministic scientific computing. No external dependencies for core functionality. No hidden sources of non-determinism. Every floating-point reduction uses compensated summation. Every collection is ordered. Every random number comes from a seeded, splittable generator.

title: Adam Ezzat
text:
What I Bring Together

Data Science & ML
Time-series forecasting, clustering, predictive modeling, neural network architectures (LSTM, N-BEATS, REINFORCE). Professional experience building and deploying models that actually ship.


Systems & Infrastructure
Data pipelines, automation, validation frameworks, database engineering. The unsexy work that makes everything else possible.


Language & Compiler Engineering
Lexer, parser, type system, HIR/MIR lowering, optimization passes, dual execution engines — all built from scratch in Rust. 20 crates, 5,300+ tests, 6.4 MB single binary.


The Full Stack of Understanding
Economics gave me the “why.” Data science gave me the “what.” Systems engineering gave me the “how.” CJC is where all three meet.

title: Adam Ezzat
text:
Where I Am Now
Professionally, I’m a Data Scientist at Big Data Insights, building production forecasting systems. Personally, I’m deep in the CJC compiler — pushing toward a stable module system, improving type inference, and writing about the process along the way.
I believe the best way to understand a system is to build it yourself. That’s true for forecasting models, data pipelines, and — it turns out — entire programming languages.

Read the blog View projects About me

title: Projects
text:
A deterministic programming language for scientific computing, built from scratch in Rust with nearly zero external dependencies.
CJC covers the full stack: lexer, parser, type checker, two intermediate representations (HIR and MIR), an optimizer, two execution engines, and a runtime with ~300 built-in functions spanning math, statistics, linear algebra, signal processing, machine learning, and data manipulation.
Key technical facts:

6.4 MB single binary — the entire language in one file
20 Rust crates, nearly zero external dependencies
5,300+ passing tests across 55 test binaries
Deterministic execution: same seed = bit-identical results







CJC has two execution paths that must always agree:

AST Interpreter (cjc-eval) — direct tree-walk execution for rapid prototyping and debugging
MIR Executor (cjc-mir-exec) — register-machine execution through HIR → MIR lowering with optimization

Parity tests verify that both engines produce identical results for every program. The MIR path supports constant folding and dead code elimination (--mir-opt), and a NoGC static verifier proves that annotated code paths never trigger garbage collection.



Unification-based type checker with explicit annotations. Comprehensive diagnostic engine with structured error codes (E0xxx–E8xxx) and rich terminal output. Language features include closures with capture analysis, match expressions with structural destructuring, for-loops, structs, enums, and tuples.







N-dimensional tensors with zero-copy COW (copy-on-write) buffers. Operations include matrix multiplication (sequential, tiled, and parallel), SVD, QR, Cholesky, eigenvalue decomposition, determinant, inverse, and norm computation.
All floating-point reductions use Kahan or binned summation to maintain numerical stability. SIMD kernels avoid FMA instructions to preserve cross-platform bit-identical results.



Forward-mode AD via dual numbers and reverse-mode AD via tape-based gradient computation. Supports standard operations (arithmetic, trigonometric, exponential, logarithmic, power) with automatic derivative propagation.
Used in practice for physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) and physics-informed machine learning (PIML) — demonstrated on heat equation and harmonic oscillator problems with verified convergence.



35+ statistical functions (mean, std, variance, correlation, covariance, percentile) and 13 probability distributions (normal, uniform, beta, gamma, binomial, chi-squared, t, Weibull). 15 hypothesis tests (t-test, chi-squared, ANOVA, Wilcoxon, Mann-Whitney). All seeded and deterministic.







73+ DataFrame operations modeled on R’s tidyverse: filter, select, mutate, group_by, summarize, arrange, pivot_longer, pivot_wider, joins (inner, left, right, anti, semi), and window functions (lag, lead, cumsum, ntile).
Supports typed columns (int, float, string, boolean, categorical, datetime), CSV ingestion, and lazy evaluation with COW semantics.







A full chess environment and REINFORCE reinforcement learning agent written entirely in CJC. 49 tests covering board invariants, legal move generation, policy gradient updates, and training determinism. Validates that CJC can express a complete ML workflow end-to-end.



PIML demonstration: 1D heat equation solved via polynomial regression with physics residual penalty. PINN demonstration: harmonic oscillator solved with a neural network surrogate. Both verified for solution accuracy, PDE residual convergence, gradient correctness, boundary condition satisfaction, and bit-identical determinism across runs.







15+ subcommands: run, check, parse, lex, repl, plus inspection (inspect, schema), analysis (trace, flow, drift), performance (bench, mem), and utility (pack, doctor, forge, patch). Supports --reproducible mode, --seed control, --mir-opt optimization, and rich diagnostic formatting.



Grammar-of-graphics visualization library built into the CJC ecosystem. Declarative plot specification following principles similar to ggplot2. Currently integrated at the library level within the CJC runtime.
